question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: '] [Function: throttleOpcodeRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the very low default opcodeRequestsPerSecond (1 RPS) to perform a denial-of-service by sending exactly 1 request per second to the opcode endpoint, monopolizing the entire opcode budget and preventing any other user from using the opcode tracing feature?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: '] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that restore() is called with the full gas value from the request (not the scaled value) in ContractController, but scaleGas() is applied inside restore(), and by crafting a gas value just above GAS_SCALE_FACTOR, cause restore() to add only 1 token back while the original throttle() consumed more, creating a token deficit that accumulates over time?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: '] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the condition in restore() — 'if (tokens > 0) gasLimitBucket.addTokens(tokens)' — by sending requests with gas <= GAS_SCALE_FACTOR so that scaleGas() returns 0 and restore() adds 0 tokens, while the original throttle() also consumed 0 tokens, creating a consistent bypass of gas accounting?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: '] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the ThrottleManager interface is public and the implementation (ThrottleManagerImpl) is package-private, but the interface is injected into ContractController, to craft requests that exercise all three methods (throttle, throttleOpcodeRequest, restore) in a sequence that leaves the throttle state inconsistent?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: '] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the RequestFilter.test() method uses Strings.CI::contains (Apache Commons Lang case-insensitive contains) which may have different Unicode normalization behavior than expected, allowing an attacker to craft Unicode-encoded data fields that bypass case-insensitive CONTAINS filters?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: '] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the FROM FilterField extracts the from address as a String and uses case-insensitive matching, allowing an attacker to use mixed-case Ethereum addresses (e.g., EIP-55 checksum addresses) to bypass REJECT filters that only match lowercase or uppercase address patterns?
